For each unit, the rate of entropy generation $\dot{S}_G$ is computed from an entropy balance, then lost work $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = T_\sigma \dot{S}_G$. Summing $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}}$ over all units and comparing with $\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}}$ or $\dot{W}_s$ reveals where inefficiencies are largest.
trap: 🚨 When heat transfer occurs across a finite temperature difference, the entropy balance uses the temperature at which heat is transferred, not the surroundings temperature, unless the heat is rejected directly to the surroundings. The lost work calculation uses $T_\sigma$ for the entropy generation from all sources.

subject: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
name: Thermodynamic Analysis of a Steam Power Plant
problem: A steam power plant operates with steam conditions as given in Example 8.1. Methane is burned with 25% excess air in the furnace/boiler. Flue gas leaves at 460 K, surroundings at 298.15 K. Perform a thermodynamic analysis: compute ideal work, entropy generation and lost work for each unit (furnace/boiler, turbine, condenser, pump), and determine the thermodynamic efficiency.
solution_steps: Step 1: Calculate properties of steam at key points (from Ex. 8.1) and combustion data. On basis of 1 mol CH4, compute ΔH and ΔS for the overall combustion + cooling process.
Step 2: From steam rate (84.75 kg/s) and ΔH per mol CH4, determine methane flow rate: 366.30 mol/s.
Step 3: Compute ideal work: $\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}} = \dot{m}_{CH4}[\Delta H - T_\sigma \Delta S] = -292.94 \times 10^3$ kW.
Step 4: For each unit, compute $\dot{S}_G$ from entropy balance, then $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = T_\sigma \dot{S}_G$.
Step 5: Furnace/boiler: $\dot{S}_G = 587.12$ kW/K → $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 175.05 \times 10^3$ kW.
Step 6: Turbine: $\dot{S}_G = 84.65$ kW/K → $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 25.24 \times 10^3$ kW.
Step 7: Condenser: $\dot{S}_G = 41.69$ kW/K → $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 12.32 \times 10^3$ kW.
Step 8: Pump: $\dot{S}_G = 0.74$ kW/K → $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 0.22 \times 10^3$ kW.
Step 9: Entropy generation distribution: furnace/boiler 82.2%, turbine 11.9%, condenser 5.8%, pump 0.1%.
Step 10: Work analysis: $|\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}}| = 292.94 \times 10^3$ kW; actual work output from Ex. 8.1 = 80.00 × 10³ kW; total lost work = 212.83 × 10³ kW; efficiency = 27.3%.
final_answer: Thermodynamic efficiency = 27.3%; major loss occurs in the furnace/boiler (59.8% of $|\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}}|$).
trap: 🚨 The ideal work calculation must account for the chemical reaction. The entropy of mixing of air and flue gas contributes significantly to $\Delta S$. The entropy generation in the furnace/boiler includes both combustion irreversibility and heat transfer across finite ΔT.

subject: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
name: Linde Liquefaction Process Analysis
problem: Methane is liquefied in a Linde system (Fig. 16.3). Methane is compressed from 1 bar, 300 K to 60 bar, cooled to 300 K, then passes through a heat exchanger and throttle to produce saturated liquid at 1 bar. Unliquefied gas returns at 295 K. A heat leak of 5 kJ/kg into the exchanger is present. Compressor work is 1000 kJ/kg with 75% efficiency. Surroundings at 300 K. Perform thermodynamic analysis: find ideal work, lost work per unit, and efficiency.
solution_steps: Step 1: Obtain properties for methane at key points from tables (provided).
Step 2: Compute liquefied fraction z from energy balance: $z = (H_6 - H_2 - Q)/(H_6 - H_4) = 0.0486$.
Step 3: Compute ideal work: $\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}} = \Delta (H\dot{m}) - T_\sigma \Delta (S\dot{m}) = 53.8$ kJ per kg of inlet methane.
Step 4: For compression/cooling unit: $\dot{Q} = H_2-H_1 - \dot{W}_s = -1059.8$ kJ/kg; $\dot{S}_G = (S_2-S_1) - \dot{Q}/T_\sigma = 1.2627$ kJ/kg·K; $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 378.8$ kJ/kg.
Step 5: For heat exchanger: $\dot{S}_G = (S_6-S_5)(1-z) + (S_3-S_2) - Q_{\text{leak}}/T_\sigma = 0.3879$ kJ/kg·K; $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 116.4$ kJ/kg.
Step 6: For throttle + separator: $\dot{S}_G = z S_4 + (1-z)S_5 - S_3 = 1.5033$ kJ/kg·K; $\dot{W}_{\text{lost}} = 451.0$ kJ/kg.
Step 7: Entropy generation distribution: compression/cooling 40.0%, exchanger 12.3%, throttle 47.7%.
Step 8: Work analysis: $\dot{W}_s = 1000$ kJ/kg, $\dot{W}_{\text{ideal}} = 53.8$ kJ/kg, total lost work = 946.2 kJ/kg; efficiency = 5.4%.
final_answer: Thermodynamic efficiency = 5.4%; major loss occurs in the throttling step (45.1% of $\dot{W}_s$).
trap: 🚨 The throttle valve is highly irreversible, producing the largest lost work. Replacing it with an expander would greatly improve efficiency but increase capital cost. Heat leak into the exchanger reduces liquefaction yield and adds to lost work.
